March ; 3, 2021
We sincerely thank the reviewers for their insightful feedback and suggestions for
improving the manuscript. Next, we present our changes corresponding to the comments
by the reviewers. We have also highlighted the revisions in the manuscript by marking
them ; in red ; in this ; response ; letter.
| ---- | --------- | ------- | -------- | ---------- | ------- | ----- | --- | --------- | --- |
1 ; Reviewer1 ; (Suggested ; Minor ; Revision)
We thank the reviewer for the insightful feedback and suggestions. Please see the
summary ; of our ; responses ; and ; revisions.
| ------- | --- | ------ | --------- | --- | ---------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
1. Comment: Regarding comment 1: The response to my first question did not
address the question. What is a query in FineStream? Pointing to your prior work
is not sufficient here. Please elaborate on types of queries early in your manuscript.
Response: Thanks for the comment from the reviewer. In this paper, a query
represents a statement from a user to view data in streams. Currently, we support
queries with five basic kinds of operators: projection, selection, aggregation, group-
by, and join. Note that more operators can be supported in the future. We have
added ; the ; following ; text ; in Section ; 1.
| --- | ----- | --- | --------- | ---- | ---------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
In this paper, a query represents a statement from a user to view data in streams.
Currently, we support queries with five basic kinds of operators: projection, selec-
tion, aggregation, group-by, and join. Note that more operators can be supported
in the ; future. ; More ; details ; are presented ; in Section ; 6.
| --- | ------ | ------- | ---- | ------- | ------------- | --- | ---------- | --- | --- |
2. Comment: Regarding comment 2: The introduction now states “Note that a dis-
crete GPU could have 10x higher computation capacity and 22x higher bandwidth.
This further validates the large potential of exploring the integrated architectures for
data stream processing.” In the context of the paragraph that contains this state-
ment, you say that FineStream can achieve lower latency than discrete architectures,
but then you say the GPU may have higher computation capacity and memory band-
width. This statement should be carefully merged into this paragraph. Doesn’t this
new ; statement ; undermine ; your work?
| --- | --- | --------- | --------- | --- | ---------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
Response: Thanks for the comment from the reviewer. Although a discrete GPU
couldhavemuchhighercomputationcapacityandmuchhigherGPUmemoryband-
width, the discrete GPU connects to the CPU and host memory via PCIe. The
PCIe bandwidth ranges from 4GB/s to 50GB/s, which is much slower than the
discrete ; GPU ; memory ; bandwidth.
| --- | -------- | --- | ------ | --- | ---------- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
1

Transactions on Parallel and Distributed Systems Page 2 of 20
In contrast, the integrated architecture fusions the CPU and the GPU on the same
chip, sharing the same physical memory, so the integrated architecture avoids PCIe
datatransmission. Therefore,wewrotethat“Underthecircumstancesthatthedata
transmission time is long and the transmission overhead exceeds the performance
benefits brought by discrete GPUs of the discrete architectures, FineStream is able
to achieve lower processing latency, compared to the state-of-the-art execution on
discrete architectures”.
Sorry for the misunderstanding about our presentation. We have updated the text
in Section 1 as follows.
When we compare FineStream on integrated architectures with stream processing
on discrete architectures, although a discrete GPU could have 10× higher computa-
tion capacity and 22× higher bandwidth than the integrated architecture, the PCIe
transmission could become its bottleneck. In contrast, the integrated architecture
avoidsPCIetransmission. Underthecircumstancesthatthedatatransmissiontime
is long and the transmission overhead exceeds the performance benefits brought by
discrete GPUs of the discrete architectures, FineStream is able to achieve lower
processing latency, compared to the state-of-the-art execution on discrete archi-
tectures. More details are shown in Section 8.4. This further validates the large
potential of exploring the integrated architectures for data stream processing.
3. Comment: Regarding comment 3: Your response did not answer my question.
What is the probability that two users will submit queries at the same time and will
benefit from sharing the same results? If the probability is very low, this should be
elaborated upon in the paper. It seems like a feature that may have little use in
practice.
Response: Thanks for the comment from the reviewer. In real-world cases, two
users can submit different queries at the same time. For example, in stock market
analysis of event stream processor, many queries can be submitted to the system
on the same data stream simultaneously, even with the interests on the same stock
patterns [30]. Hence, the probability that the stock market stream system processes
multiple queries at the same time is very high. We have added the following text
in Section 6.1 as follows.
FineStream detects the workload changes and periodically applies sharing-aware
query optimization. When two users submit queries simultaneously, the operator
sharing optimization still can be applied. In real-world cases, two users can submit
different queries at the same time. For example, in stock market analysis of event
stream processor, many queries can be submitted to the system on the same data
stream simultaneously, even with the interests on the same stock patterns [30].
Hence, the probability that the stock market stream system processes multiple
queries at the same time is very high.
